Where South Sudanese Soil Meets Commercial Purpose

Nuru — meaning "light" — is a commercial agribusiness farm growing vegetables, fruits, and livestock across 36–50 acres in Southern Luri. The farm supplies fresh produce to Juba's growing market year-round.

About Nuru

Commercial farming built for Juba's food economy

Nuru Agribusiness Farm is a privately owned commercial farm operating in Southern Luri, Central Equatoria — one of South Sudan's most agriculturally suitable zones. The name Nuru means "light." The farm produces vegetables, fruits, and goats across a planned 36 to 50 acres of cultivated land.

Southern Luri's climate — reliable rainfall, fertile alluvial soil, and proximity to the Nile basin — makes it suitable for year-round cultivation without heavy irrigation costs. The farm targets direct supply to Juba's retail and wholesale markets, reducing the city's dependence on imported produce from Uganda and Kenya.

Nuru operates as a for-profit agribusiness. Profit reinvestment funds each subsequent growth phase, soil improvement, and workforce expansion.

Commercial focus

Every crop grown is matched to documented Juba market demand before planting.

Local employment

Farm staff are hired from Southern Luri and surrounding communities.

Soil stewardship

Composting and crop rotation are standard practice, not optional add-ons.

Reinvestment

Phase 2 expansion is funded entirely from Phase 1 operating surplus.

Southern Luri

Farm location
📍

Address

Southern Luri, Juba, Central Equatoria, South Sudan

🌱

Land area

36 acres (Phase 1) expanding to 50 acres (Phase 2)

🌦️

Climate advantage

Bimodal rainfall pattern supports two full growing cycles per year

🛣️

Market access

Direct road access to Juba main market, minimising post-harvest losses

What we grow

Three product categories

All production targets Juba's fresh-produce market. Varieties are selected for heat tolerance, market price, and shelf life under ambient storage conditions.

🥬

Vegetables

Primary cash crop

High-demand staples sold to Juba retailers, hotels, and wholesale buyers. Tomatoes and onions are the highest-volume lines. Carrots and cabbage provide crop diversity and rotation benefits.

🍅 Tomatoes 🧅 Onions 🥕 Carrots 🥬 Cabbage
🥑

Fruits

Long-cycle perennials

Perennial crops planted in Phase 1 for Phase 2 harvest returns. Dragon fruit, avocado, and banana occupy distinct land zones. Avocado and dragon fruit carry strong export potential to regional markets.

🥑 Avocado 🍌 Banana 🐉 Dragon fruit
🐐

Livestock

Diversified income

Goat farming provides a secondary income stream with fast breeding cycles and strong Juba market demand. Goat manure also supports on-farm composting, reducing input costs for vegetable production.

🐐 Boer goats 🐐 Local breeds
How we operate

Farm operations and management

Nuru runs on a planned-production model: planting schedules, harvest cycles, and delivery logistics are coordinated to maintain consistent market supply.

Cultivation approach

Field management
  • Seasonal planting calendar aligned to Juba's two rainy seasons
  • Raised beds for vegetables to improve drainage and reduce soil erosion
  • Drip irrigation infrastructure planned for dry-season production
  • Composted goat manure used as primary soil amendment
  • Integrated pest management to reduce chemical input costs

Market and distribution

Sales and logistics
  • Direct supply agreements with Juba wholesale buyers and hotels
  • Farm gate sales open daily during harvest periods
  • Refrigerated transport sourced for tomato and leafy vegetable delivery
  • Pricing benchmarked against Konyo Konyo market weekly rates

Workforce

People and skills
  • Permanent farm manager based on-site year-round
  • Seasonal labour hired from Southern Luri and adjacent communities
  • Technical training in post-harvest handling and storage
  • Succession planning tied to Phase 2 workforce scale-up

Financial structure

Capital and reinvestment
  • Phase 1 funded through founder capital and early investor participation
  • Phase 2 land acquisition and infrastructure financed from Phase 1 surplus
  • Annual audited accounts maintained from Year 1
  • Board oversight on all capital expenditure above USD 5,000
Who we are

Leadership and governance

Nhial Jool Pat But

Founder & Chief Executive Officer

Nhial founded Nuru Agribusiness Farm to develop a commercially viable agricultural operation in Southern Luri. He leads overall strategy, investor relations, and business development.

Education Bachelor of Business Administration, Anadolu University, Türkiye

Board of Directors

Nuru is governed by a five-member board with oversight responsibilities in finance, agronomy, market strategy, legal compliance, and community relations. The board meets quarterly and approves all major capital and strategic decisions.

Our mission

"To build a productive, commercially sustainable farm that supplies quality food to Juba's market, generates employment in Southern Luri, and demonstrates that South Sudanese land can power South Sudanese enterprise."

Growth plan

Two-phase development

Nuru scales deliberately. Phase 1 establishes core vegetable and livestock production. Phase 2 expands acreage and adds fruit orchard returns.

01
Active — Year 1–2

Foundation & Production Launch

36 acres · Southern Luri

Phase 1 puts the farm into commercial production. Vegetable plots are prepared and planted, goat herd is established, and the first harvest cycles run. Sales channels into Juba's market are opened and tested. Operating systems, workforce, and financial records are built to the standard Phase 2 requires.

Tomatoes Onions Carrots Cabbage Goat herd (initial) Perennial planting begins
02
Planned — Year 3–5

Expansion & Fruit Returns

50 acres · Full capacity

Phase 2 adds 14 acres to reach the 50-acre target and brings fruit orchard production online. Avocado, banana, and dragon fruit planted in Phase 1 reach commercial harvest volumes. The goat herd is expanded. Infrastructure upgrades — cold storage, improved irrigation — are funded from Phase 1 surplus.

Avocado Banana Dragon fruit Expanded vegetable beds Cold storage unit Goat herd (expanded)
